shower.thought: We assume our childhood shaped us, but maybe we just remember the parts that matched who we were already becoming.
The only time you truly know how someone else experiences the world is the moment you stop trying to understand it.
You can't be nostalgic for a time when you were actually happy—you can only be nostalgic for how you felt remembering it later.
Every person you've forgotten probably remembers you.
The things you regret most are usually the versions of events that never actually happened.
You spend your whole life looking for people who get you, never realizing that being understood might be less important than being accepted anyway.
Comfort is just another word for the rut you've decided is worth it.
The people who seem most confident are often just better at ignoring their own contradictions.
You learn more about yourself from what bores you than from what excites you.
Your taste in music is really just a timeline of who you wanted to be.
We're all pretending the future will be different from the past while taking the exact same actions.
The worst part about getting older isn't losing energy—it's realizing how much of your youth you spent waiting.
You only think you have a type in people because you've only seriously known a handful of them.
Silence reveals what people actually think; words are just what they've decided is safe to say.
The people closest to you are often the ones you understand the least.
You can't miss someone you never actually knew.
The things you're most defensive about are usually the things you're not sure about yet.
Your childhood trauma and your greatest strength might actually be the same thing wearing different names.
Most of your opinions aren't really yours—they're just the ones you haven't questioned yet.
We treat our potential like it's a resource we can save, when really it only exists in the using.

motivational.thought:
The person who says it cannot be done should not interrupt the person doing it.
— Chinese Proverb

Your potential is endless, but your time is not. Choose what matters.
— Unknown

Mastery is not about perfection. It's about progression.
— Unknown

The only way to do impossible work is to believe it matters.
— Unknown

You cannot control the outcome, but you can control the effort. Give everything to the effort.
— Unknown

Comfort is the enemy of growth. Lean into what scares you.
— Unknown

Decades from now, you won't remember the days you rested. You'll remember the days you pushed.
— Unknown

The difference between who you are and who you want to be is what you do.
— Unknown

Success whispers while failure screams. Learn to listen to the whispers.
— Unknown

Every expert was once a beginner who refused to quit.
— Unknown

Progress over perfection, always. The imperfect action beats the perfect plan.
— Unknown

Your future self is watching you right now. Don't let them down.
— Unknown

Consistency is what separates dreamers from achievers.
— Unknown

The cost of discipline is nothing compared to the cost of regret.
— Unknown

You are not behind. You are not late. You are exactly where you need to be.
— Unknown

What lies behind you and what lies before you matter little compared to what lies within you.
— Ralph Waldo Emerson

Doubt kills more dreams than failure ever will.
— Unknown

The moment you stop making excuses is the moment your life changes.
— Unknown

What you resist persists. What you acknowledge and accept, you can transform.
— Carl Jung

Your struggle today is building the strength you'll need tomorrow.
— Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
I told my girlfriend I needed space.
She said, "Good idea," and started packing.

My doctor said I have six months to live.
I said I can't afford the treatment.
He said, "Then you have three months."

I'm at that age where my back goes out more than I do.
Last week my back went out with my wife.
I'm still not sure which one I'm angrier about.

My therapist asked what my biggest fear is.
I said, "Abandonment."
He said, "We can pick this up next week," and left for vacation.

I tried to tell my kids about the financial crisis of 2008.
They said, "We lived through 2020, 2021, 2022, and 2023."
Apparently, we've just been in a constant financial crisis and nobody told me.

My wife asked if I still love her as much as the day we met.
I said, "Absolutely."
She smiled. I didn't mention that I was miserable that day too.

The dentist told me I need to floss more.
I said, "Will it help?"
He said, "No, but I'll feel better about myself."

I asked my boss if there was any job security in my position.
He said, "Absolutely. You're secure that you have a job."
I asked for how long.
He said, "That's between you and God now."

My kid asked me if Santa was real.
I said yes, and he's about to ruin Christmas.
He's starting college in the fall.

I went to a self-help seminar on letting go.
The speaker said, "You have to release your pain."
I said, "How?"
He said, "However you want. I don't care anymore."

My friend said he's been sober for a year.
I said, "That's amazing!"
He said, "I know. I've never been more aware of how much I don't like you."

I asked my accountant if I should be worried about my investments.
He said, "Only if you want to sleep at night."
I said, "I haven't done that in years anyway."
He said, "Then we're fine."

My mother-in-law said she'd like to spend more time with us.
My wife got excited.
I started updating my résumé for jobs in other countries.

I told my friend his marriage was falling apart.
He said, "I know. It's the best thing that's happened all year."
I said, "It's only January."
He said, "Exactly."

My kid asked why people get married if half of them end in divorce.
I said, "Hope."
He said, "That's just denial with commitment issues."
I said, "Now you're getting it."

I went to my high school reunion.
Everyone asked what I'd been up to.
Apparently, "Disappointing my parents" isn't as impressive as I thought.

My psychiatrist asked if I ever think about killing myself.
I said, "Only when I'm awake."
He said, "And when you're asleep?"
I said, "Then I'm actually happy."
He increased my prescription.

I asked a homeless man how he ended up on the streets.
He said, "Bad decisions."
I said, "Me too," and we both laughed.
Then I got in my car and he stayed.

My wife said she needed to find herself.
I said, "Did you check the kitchen?"
She's been gone for three weeks.
Turns out I was onto something.

My boss asked why I've been so quiet lately.
I said, "I'm just observing."
He said, "Observing what?"
I said, "How replaceable I am."
